text: “Excuse me,” I said, breaking in, “but can you show me one of this kind? It is something new to me. I must examine it. I shall scour the town until I come upon one. Its habitat must be here on Broadway.”
source_text: “Excuse me,” I interrupted, “but can you produce one of this type? It is a new thing to me. I must study it. I will search the town over until I find one. Its habitat must be here on Broadway.”

text: “But she has a will—not to mention a temper—and I shall be nothing better than a slave to her. I could do anything at all with poor Fanny Robin.”
source_text: “But she has a will—not to say a temper, and I shall be a mere slave to her. I could do anything with poor Fanny Robin.”

text:
You left for me the bounds of pain,
Vast as the heaving sea,
Set betwixt eternity and time,
Your mind, and mine, and me.
source_text:
You left me boundaries of pain
Capacious as the sea,
Between eternity and time,
Your consciousness and me.

text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin replied, with a shake of the hand and a motion toward the lone chair, while he himself seated himself upon the bed. “Yet how came you to know where I lived?”
source_text: “No, no, not at all,” Martin answered, shaking hands and waving him to the solitary chair, himself taking to the bed. “But how did you know where I lived?”

text: Previous work has employed noising methods based on random n-gram modifications (Fevry and Phang, 2018); however, we instead depend on two straightforward, linguistically motivated noise functions. First, we train a bidirectional language model (BiLM; Peters et al., 2018) on the review corpus C. For each word in y, the BiLM produces a softmax distribution over the vocabulary, which can then be used to substitute words. Second, we use FLAIR 1 (Akbik et al., 2019), an off-the-shelf state-of-the-art syntactic chunker that exploits contextual embeddings, to shallow-parse each review r in corpus C. This yields a list of chunks C_r = {c_1, ..., c_K} together with the corresponding syntactic labels G_r = {g_1, ..., g_K} for each review r, which we use to replace and reorder chunks.
source_text: N }. Previous work has adopted noising techniques based on random n-gram alterations (Fevry and Phang, 2018) , however, we instead rely on two simple, linguistically informed noise functions. Firstly, we train a bidirectional language model (BiLM; Peters et al., 2018) on the review corpus C. For each word in y, the BiLM predicts a softmax word distribution which can be used to replace words. Secondly, we utilize FLAIR 1 (Akbik et al., 2019) , an off-theshelf state-of-the-art syntactic chunker that leverages contextual embeddings, to shallow parse each review r in corpus C. This results in a list of chunks C r = {c 1 , ..., c K } with corresponding syntactic labels G r = {g 1 , ..., g K } for each review r, which we use for replacing and rearranging chunks.

text:
May sorrow seize the man sae mean,  
Though bright his purse wi’ coin be clean,  
Wha follows ony saucy lass  
That walks sae proud and high.
source_text:
But sorrow tak’ him that’s sae mean,
Altho’ his pouch o’ coin were clean,
Wha follows ony saucy quean,
That looks sae proud and high.
O Tibbie, I hae seen the day, &c.

text: “Ay—so ’a do seem, Billy Smallbury—so ’a do seem.” The words were in themselves greatly unsteady, and by reason of the circumstance they were rendered yet more so, for the jolting of the waggon was not without effect upon the speaker’s larynx. They came from the man who held the reins.
source_text: “Ay—so ’a do seem, Billy Smallbury—so ’a do seem.” This utterance was very shaky by nature, and more so by circumstance, the jolting of the waggon not being without its effect upon the speaker’s larynx. It came from the man who held the reins.

text: “Is that all, my dear Henry? How could you suppose that my first thought would not fly towards those dear, dear friends whom I love and who are so deserving of my love?”
“There!” he said, setting the bundle down on the table. “See to that, and do as much as you can with it. It was trouble enough to get; I had thought I ought to have been here three hours ago.”
source_text: “There!” he said, laying the bundle on the table. “Take care of that, and do the most you can with it. It’s been trouble enough to get; I thought I should have been here, three hours ago.”

text:
Far from love the Heavenly Father
Leads the chosen child;
Oftener through realm of briar
Than the meadow mild,

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
As they come unto an end,
Of that which lies beneath the earlier songs—of my intent in them,
Of the seed I have striven to sow in them,
Of joy, sweet joy, through many a year, in them,
(For them, for them have I lived, in them my task is done,)
Of many a dear aspiration, of many a dream and design;
Through Space and Time blended in a chant, and the flowing eternal sameness,
To Nature, enfolding these, enfolding God—to the joyous,
electric all,
To the thought of Death, and taking up exultant Death in its turn
as one with life,
The advent of man to sing;
To draw together you, ye parted, diverse lives,
To make accord of mountains and rocks and streams,
And the winds of the north, and the forests of oak and pine,
With you O soul.
source_text:
As they draw to a close,
Of what underlies the precedent songs--of my aims in them,
Of the seed I have sought to plant in them,
Of joy, sweet joy, through many a year, in them,
(For them, for them have I lived, in them my work is done,)
Of many an aspiration fond, of many a dream and plan;
Through Space and Time fused in a chant, and the flowing eternal identity,
To Nature encompassing these, encompassing God--to the joyous,
electric all,
To the sense of Death, and accepting exulting in Death in its turn
the same as life,
The entrance of man to sing;
To compact you, ye parted, diverse lives,
To put rapport the mountains and rocks and streams,
And the winds of the north, and the forests of oak and pine,
With you O soul.

text: This work addresses multiple open questions about syntactic evaluations and their relationship to other language model assessments. Our results dissociate model perplexity and performance in syntactic generalization tests, suggesting that the two metrics capture complementary features of language model knowledge. In a controlled evaluation of different model classes and datasets, we find model architecture plays a more important role than training data scale in yielding correct syntactic generalizations. Our circuit-level analysis reveals consistent failure on Licensing but inconsistent behavior on other circuits, suggesting that different syntactic circuits make use of different underlying processing capacities. In addition to the insight these results provide about neural NLP systems, they also bear on questions central to cognitive science and linguistics, putting lower bounds on what syntactic knowledge can be acquired from string input alone.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

Not for naught have I brought thee hither—we must remain,

source_text: “And how is she getting on without a baily?” the maltster inquired. Henery shook his head, and smiled one of the bitter smiles, dragging all the flesh of his forehead into a corrugated heap in the centre.

text: “Yes, yes, I suppose that is about the extent of it. Do not be troubled by my seeming excited; I am not. Let us turn the subject. Now, as for this castle, with forty-five princesses in it and three ogres at its head, tell me—where is this harem?”
source_text: “Yes, yes, I reckon that's about the size of it. Don't mind my seeming excited; I'm not. Let us change the subject. Now as to this castle, with forty-five princesses in it, and three ogres at the head of it, tell me--where is this harem?”

text: “I agreed to it,” Thomasin replied with firmness. “I am a practical woman now. I put no faith in hearts whatsoever. I would marry him under any circumstances since—since Clym’s letter.”
source_text: “I agreed to it,” Thomasin answered firmly. “I am a practical woman now. I don’t believe in hearts at all. I would marry him under any circumstances since—since Clym’s letter.”

text: Other approaches are similar to ours in the sense that they utilize the structure provided by interview prompts. Al Hanai et al. (2018) and Gong and Poellabauer (2017) propose models that extract separate sets of features for responses to each unique prompt in their corpus. However, these approaches require manually identifying unique prompts. Our model can instead automatically learn new, task-specific categorization of prompts. Lubis et al. (2018) perform a K-means clustering of prompt to assign prompts to latent dialogue act categories. These are used as features in a neural dialogue system. Our approach expands upon this idea of incorporating a separate unsupervised clustering step by allowing the learning goal to influence the clustering. Our approach is also related to that of Chaturvedi et al. (2014) in that it automatically categorizes various parts of the conversation. However, they use domain-specific handcrafted features and discrete latent variables for this categorization. Our approach instead can leverage the neural architecture to automatically identify features useful for this categorization.

metadata.title: Predicting Depression in Screening Interviews from Latent Categorization of Interview Prompts

text: We introduce the KLEJ benchmark, a comprehensive set of evaluation tasks for the Polish language understanding. Its goal is to drive the development of better NLU models, so careful selection of tasks was crucial. We mainly focused on a variety of text genres, objectives, text lengths, and difficulties, which allows us to assess the models across different axes. As a result, KLEJ benchmark proves to be both challenging and diverse, as there is no single model that outperforms others on all tasks.

metadata.title: KLEJ: Comprehensive Benchmark for Polish Language Understanding

I spoke my rage, and then it ended.
I was angry with my foe:
I kept it in, and my rage kept growing.

I was angry with my friend:
I told my wrath, my wrath did end.
I was angry with my foe:
I told it not, my wrath did grow.

text: “My wife appears to believe I’m a burglar,” he said, with reckless composure. “I never heard anyone call her mad; so it must be I. And if I am mad, then they can’t do anything to me for killing you two fools in my madness.”
source_text: “My wife seems to think I am a burglar,” he said, recklessly. “I never heard of her being crazy; therefore I must be. And if I’m crazy, they can’t do anything to me for killing you two fools in my madness.”

The bolt across the door,  
To seek the guest made perfect, --  
Her visitor no more.

The bolt upon the door,
To seek for the accomplished guest, --
Her visitor no more.

source_text: “As weak as water,” replied Mr. Sikes, with an imprecation on his eyes and limbs. “Here; lend us a hand, and let me get off this thundering bed anyhow.”

text: Having made, at least, this one hit, whatever it might prove to be worth, and no customers coming in to help him to any other, Mr. Barsad paid for what he had drunk, and took his leave: taking occasion to say, in a genteel manner, before he departed, that he looked forward to the pleasure of seeing Monsieur and Madame Defarge again. For some minutes after he had emerged into the outer presence of Saint Antoine, the husband and wife remained exactly as he had left them, lest he should come back.

Then from that solitude the sceptred wretch  
I drew, and, moved to pity at his change,  
With sorrowing words allay’d his savage mood.  
Yet he, while pride and fear in conflict strange  
Contended deep, with morose craft of hate  
Ill-hid, did glare on me, as doth a snake  
Toothless, yet venomous in its bent estate.  
Not scorn, but pity, in my breast did wake,  
Though desolate the desolator stood, and unaware  
The curses which he mocked had seized him by the hair.
source_text:
The sceptred wretch then from that solitude
I drew, and, of his change compassionate,
With words of sadness soothed his rugged mood.
But he, while pride and fear held deep debate,
With sullen guile of ill-dissembled hate
Glared on me as a toothless snake might glare:
Pity, not scorn I felt, though desolate
The desolator now, and unaware
The curses which he mocked had caught him by the hair.

text: “I went to Bath that night fully resolved to break my engagement with Mr. Troy. It was by reason of circumstances that arose after my arrival there that—that we were married. Now, do you not regard the matter in a different light?”
source_text: “I went to Bath that night in the full intention of breaking off my engagement to Mr. Troy. It was owing to circumstances which occurred after I got there that—that we were married. Now, do you see the matter in a new light?”

The widest earth that springs
Is not so vast as that full breast

text:
But I, my life surveying, now in closing,  
With naught to show, from out its idle years to devise,  
No houses nor lands, nor signs of gems or gold for my friends,  
Yet certain remembrances of the war for you, and after you,  
And little keepsakes of camps and soldiers, with my love,  
I bind them all and leave them in this bundle of songs.
source_text:
But I, my life surveying, closing,
With nothing to show to devise from its idle years,
Nor houses nor lands, nor tokens of gems or gold for my friends,
Yet certain remembrances of the war for you, and after you,
And little souvenirs of camps and soldiers, with my love,
I bind together and bequeath in this bundle of songs.

text: Task definitions. Our rich graph-based annotation scheme allows for a number of information extraction tasks. In the scope of this paper, we address the following steps of (1) identifying sentences that describe SOFC-related experiments, recognizing and typing relevant named entities, and (3) extracting slot fillers from these sentences. The originally annotated graph structures would also allow for modeling as relations or dependency structures. We leave this to future work. The setup of our tasks is based on the assumption that in most cases, one sentence describes a single experiment. The validity of this assumption is supported by the observation that in almost all sentences containing more than one EXPERIMENT, experiment-evoking verbs actually describe variations of the same experiment. (For details on our analysis of links between experiments, see Supplementary Material Section B.) In our automatic modeling, we treat slot types as entity-types-incontext, which is a valid approximation for information extraction purposes. We leave the tasks of deciding whether two experiments are the same (same exp) or whether they constitute a variation (exp variation) to future work. While our dataset provides a good starting point, tackling these tasks will likely require collecting additional data.

metadata.title: The SOFC-Exp Corpus and Neural Approaches to Information Extraction in the Materials Science Domain

text:
Wake the serpent not—lest he
Lose the trail he should pursue,—
Let him creep while still he slumbers
Through the meadow's deep green dew!
Not a bee shall hear him sliding,
Not a may-fly lift or waken
From its bluebell cradle shaken,
Not the starlight, while he passes
Through the grass with quiet gliding.
source_text:
Wake the serpent not—lest he
Should not know the way to go,—
Let him crawl which yet lies sleeping
Through the deep grass of the meadow!
Not a bee shall hear him creeping,
Not a may-fly shall awaken
From its cradling blue-bell shaken,
Not the starlight as he’s sliding
Through the grass with silent gliding.

To ride the clouds and winds with me, and play with the boundless light.

The birds jocoser sung;

source_text: “You haven’t a penny, Mart, and that’s why you’re walkin’. Exercise!” She tried to sniff contemptuously, but succeeded in producing only a sniffle. “Here, lemme see.”

The angels sure descried,  
For I could never seek her  
On the frail, mortal side.

Her little figure at the gate
The angels must have spied,
Since I could never find her
Upon the mortal side.

source_text: “My man, don’t you be afraid,” said the dealer in red, suddenly becoming gentle. “I forgot you were here. That’s only a curious way reddlemen have of going mad for a moment; but they don’t hurt anybody. And what did the lady say then?”

text: Limiting Behavior Another important observation is that importance-sampled estimates of perplexity are consistent, that is, they converge as the number of samples tends to infinity. To establish this, we first note that p(x) is consistent, a standard consequence of the strong law of large numbers (Geweke, 1989). Consequently, log p(x) is also consistent by the continuous mapping theorem (Van der Vaart, 2000).
source_text: Limiting Behavior Another important observation is that importance sampled estimates of perplexity are consistent, e.g., will converge as the number of samples approaches infinity. To prove this, we first observe thatp(x) is consistent, which is a well-known consequence of the strong law of large numbers (Geweke, 1989) . Accordingly, logp(x) is also consistent due to the continuous mapping theorem (Van der Vaart, 2000) .

text: Model parameters are identical across the edit model and generation model, tuned on validation data. Encoders have hidden dimension 64, the decoder has hidden dimension 128, and the dimension for code and comment embeddings is 64. The embeddings used in the edit model are initialized using the pre-trained embedding vectors from the generation model. We use a dropout rate of 0.6, a batch size of 100, an initial learning rate of 0.001, and Adam optimizer. Models are trained to minimize negative log likelihood, and we terminate training if the validation loss does not decrease for ten consecutive epochs. During inference, we use beam search with beam width=20.

metadata.title: Learning to Update Natural Language Comments Based on Code Changes

source_text: “Well, if there hadn't been any Queen Guenever, it wouldn't have come so early; but it would have come, anyway. It would have come on your own account by and by; by luck, it happened to come on the queen's.”

text:

To fail with shore in view,  
Than reach my blue peninsula  
And die of bliss anew.
source_text:
It might be easier
To fail with land in sight,
Than gain my blue peninsula

The fable of heaven points to the soul,  
The soul is always lovely, it seems more or it seems less, it  
advances or it falls behind,  
It steps from its sheltered garden and gazes kindly on itself  
and gathers the world within it,  
Complete and pure the genitals once spurting, and complete and  
pure the womb adhering,  
The head full-grown, balanced and true, and the bowels and  
joints balanced and true.
source_text:
The myth of heaven indicates the soul,
The soul is always beautiful, it appears more or it appears less, it
comes or it lags behind,
It comes from its embower’d garden and looks pleasantly on itself
and encloses the world,
Perfect and clean the genitals previously jetting,and perfect and
clean the womb cohering,
The head well-grown proportion’d and plumb, and the bowels and
joints proportion’d and plumb.

“The child,” said the girl, glancing up all at once, “is better where he lies than among us; and if no mischief come to Bill of it, I hope he lies dead in the ditch, and that his young bones may rot there.”
source_text: “The child,” said the girl, suddenly looking up, “is better where he is, than among us; and if no harm comes to Bill from it, I hope he lies dead in the ditch and that his young bones may rot there.”

text: “My husband, it is indeed so. I fear he cannot now be brought back; there is hardly a hope that anything in his nature or his fortunes can be mended at this point. And yet I am certain he is capable of good things, of tender things, even of magnanimous things.”
source_text: “My husband, it is so. I fear he is not to be reclaimed; there is scarcely a hope that anything in his character or fortunes is reparable now. But, I am sure that he is capable of good things, gentle things, even magnanimous things.”

text: “Maybe he’s made of different stuff from what it takes to wear them,” said Gabriel grimly. “Well, that’s enough of this. Go on, Cainy—quick.”
source_text: “Perhaps he’s made of different stuff than to wear ’em,” said Gabriel, grimly. “Well, that’s enough of this. Go on, Cainy—quick.”

text: My days were passed in close observance, that I might more swiftly master the tongue; and I may boast I advanced more rapidly than the Arabian, who understood but little and spake in broken accents, whilst I comprehended and could counterfeit almost every word that was uttered.
source_text: “My days were spent in close attention, that I might more speedily master the language; and I may boast that I improved more rapidly than the Arabian, who understood very little and conversed in broken accents, whilst I comprehended and could imitate almost every word that was spoken.

text: “Now, Clarence, my boy—if that should chance to be your name—I’ll ask you to enlighten me a little, if you please. What is the name of that apparition which brought me here?”
source_text: “Now, Clarence, my boy--if that might happen to be your name --I'll get you to post me up a little if you don't mind. What is the name of that apparition that brought me here?”

text: “Why don’t you go away, Martin?” Gertrude had pleaded. “Go off and find work somewhere, and settle yourself down. Then, when all this has blown over, you can come back.”
source_text: “Why don’t you go away, Martin?” Gertrude had begged. “Go away and get a job somewhere and steady down. Afterwards, when this all blows over, you can come back.”

text: “If I did not take my seat, the man would perceive that we were but pretending to be equals—and, what is more, playing our part in the deception very ill indeed.”
source_text: “If I did not sit, the man would perceive that we were only pretending to be equals--and playing the deception pretty poorly, too.”

text: “Please do not speak so recklessly. When we behold such an hour draw near, we shall say, ‘I have outlived my faith and purpose,’ and die. There, the time has passed—now let us go on.”
source_text: “Please don’t say such reckless things. When we see such a time at hand we will say, ‘I have outlived my faith and purpose,’ and die. There, the hour has expired—now let us walk on.”

text:
It was later when the summer left  
Than when the cricket came,  
And yet we knew that quiet clock  
Spoke only of going home.
source_text:
'T WAS later when the summer went
Than when the cricket came,
And yet we knew that gentle clock
Meant nought but going home.

text: Before many more conjectures could be entertained, Yeobright had drawn near; and, seeing the little hair-cutting company, he turned aside to join them. Striding up, and for a moment examining their faces with a critical look, he said, without any introduction, “Now, folks, let me guess what you have been talking about.”
source_text: Before many more surmises could be indulged in Yeobright had come near; and seeing the hair-cutting group he turned aside to join them. Marching up, and looking critically at their faces for a moment, he said, without introduction, “Now, folks, let me guess what you have been talking about.”

text: Thomasin gathered herself after her first astonishment and took the packet. “That is all, ma’am—I wish you good night,” he said, and was gone from her sight.
source_text: Thomasin recovered from her surprise, and took the packet. “That’s all, ma’am—I wish you good night,” he said, and vanished from her view.

text: “No, the trenches have disappointed me. They present nothing but an irksome sort of domestic life. They are not to be compared with our quarters in the rear. There is no traffic to speak of. I have not discovered so much as one excuse for discharging my rifle. I do not think I shall ever discharge my rifle at an enemy—ever....
source_text: "No, the trenches have disappointed me. They are a scene of tiresome domesticity. They aren't a patch on our quarters in the rear. There isn't the traffic. I've not found a single excuse for firing my rifle. I don't believe I shall ever fire my rifle at an enemy--ever....

text:
“This day we mean, this very hour, to ride  
Three leagues away, upon the Apennine;  
Come down, we pray thee, ere the burning tide  
Of sun shall tell his dewy beads on vine and eglantine.”

Lorenzo, as his courtesy was still,  
Bent to that serpent-whisper with a sign;  
Then swiftly went, in haste to make him fit,  
With belt, and spur, and huntsman’s gear made tight.
source_text:
"To-day we purpose, ay, this hour we mount
To spur three leagues towards the Apennine;
Come down, we pray thee, ere the hot sun count
His dewy rosary on the eglantine."
Lorenzo, courteously as he was wont,
Bow'd a fair greeting to these serpents' whine;
And went in haste, to get in readiness,
With belt, and spur, and bracing huntsman's dress.

text:
And I have dreamed that the aim and heart of the life we know,  
the passing,  
is to shape and determine the self of the life unseen, the lasting.
source_text:
And I have dream’d that the purpose and essence of the known life,
the transient,
Is to form and decide identity for the unknown life, the permanent.
